authorship_evidence: Created by OWASP contributors Glenn and Riccardo ten Cate for a DefDev security workshop, pre-LLM era project with consistent human coding style

evidence.description: SQL injection via string concatenation: db.execute('SELECT pageId, title, content FROM pages WHERE pageId='+pageId). The pageId parameter comes directly from the URL path /dashboard/<pageId> without any sanitization or parameterization.

Server-side template injection via the 404 error handler. The request URL is injected into an HTML template string using Python .format() on line 94, then rendered via render_template_string() on line 95. An attacker can inject Jinja2 template expressions (e.g. {{7*7}}) in the URL to achieve RCE. Combined with the system_call context processor from Monitoring.py, this enables direct OS command execution.

Reflected XSS in search results page. The search parameter is rendered with autoescape explicitly disabled: {% autoescape false %}{{search}}{% endautoescape %}. The controller (Search.py) also sets X-XSS-Protection: 0 to disable browser XSS filters, making exploitation trivial.

Stored XSS via the link field in messaging. User-supplied link values are stored in the database and rendered as href attributes: <a target="_blank" href="{{item[3]}}">. While Jinja2 autoescaping prevents HTML injection in the attribute value, a javascript: URI (e.g. javascript:alert(document.cookie)) does not contain HTML special characters and passes through autoescaping, enabling XSS when the link is clicked.

Insecure YAML deserialization. User-supplied base64-encoded input from the URL path is decoded and passed to yaml.load() without specifying a safe Loader. PyYAML 3.13 (pinned in requirements.txt) defaults to the unsafe FullLoader/Loader which allows arbitrary Python object instantiation, enabling remote code execution via crafted YAML payloads (e.g. !!python/object/apply:os.system).

Insecure pickle deserialization. User-supplied hex-encoded data from POST form field 'data_obj' is written to a file (pickle.hacker) and then loaded via pickle.load(). Python's pickle module can execute arbitrary code during deserialization via __reduce__ methods, enabling RCE.

XML External Entity (XXE) injection. User-supplied XML from the 'customers' form field is parsed via xml.dom.pulldom.parseString() without disabling external entity processing. An attacker can define external entities to read local files (e.g. <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">) or perform SSRF.

Path traversal / arbitrary file read. The 'example_file' POST parameter is used directly in open(fileName, 'r') and send_file(fileName) without any path validation or sanitization. An attacker can supply '../../../etc/passwd' or any absolute path to read arbitrary files from the server.

Insecure file upload. The uploaded filename is used directly without werkzeug.utils.secure_filename() sanitization: file.save('uploads/'+filename). An attacker can use path traversal in the filename (e.g. ../../../etc/cron.d/backdoor) to write files to arbitrary locations. The allowed_file() extension check can be bypassed with double extensions or null bytes in older Python versions.

Hardcoded admin credentials in source code. The login function checks if password == 'admin' and username == 'admin', meaning the admin credentials are plaintext in the source code and trivially guessable.

Username enumeration via differential error messages. When the username is wrong, the error is 'invalid username' (line 19). When the username is correct but the password is wrong, the error is 'invalid password for username' (line 21). This allows an attacker to determine which usernames exist.

Overly permissive CORS configuration. CORS is configured with origins='*' allowing any domain to make cross-origin requests to all endpoints (r'/*'). Combined with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true set in Confidential.py, this enables cross-origin credential theft.

CORS misconfiguration with credentials. The confidential endpoint sets Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. Combined with the wildcard CORS origin in runserver.py, this allows a malicious site to make authenticated cross-origin requests and steal confidential salary data.

Weak hardcoded Flask SECRET_KEY set to the string 'random'. This secret is used to sign session cookies. An attacker who knows this value can forge session cookies, including setting session['loggedin'] to bypass authentication and access confidential pages.

Flask debug mode enabled in production (app.debug = True). This exposes the Werkzeug interactive debugger which can execute arbitrary Python code on the server if the debugger PIN is obtained or if it's not PIN-protected. It also leaks detailed stack traces and source code on errors.

Authentication bypass via hidden backdoor endpoint. The /webmaster route sets session['loggedin'] to a UUID value without requiring any authentication. Any user who navigates to /webmaster automatically gets an authenticated session, bypassing the login page entirely. This grants access to all authenticated-only pages (confidential, validator, monitoring).

OS command execution exposed via Jinja2 context processor. The system_call() function wrapping os.popen(command) is registered as a global template context processor, making it available in all templates. While the monitoring template uses it with hardcoded commands, the SSTI vulnerability in the 404 handler (defdev-app-002) allows an attacker to call system_call() with arbitrary commands, achieving full RCE.

Plaintext credentials exposed in HTML template. The webmaster page displays a table with usernames and passwords in plaintext (Mark/Thisismysecretpass, Jacob/BirdLover1, Larry/Letmein123) and secret IDs. This page is accessible to anyone who visits /webmaster, which also bypasses authentication.

Sensitive system information disclosure. The monitoring template executes and displays the output of 'ps aux | grep 8081' (process listing) and 'cat /etc/passwd' (system user accounts) directly in the page. This exposes internal system details to any authenticated user.

Cross-Site Request Forgery on message posting. The /messaging/new endpoint accepts POST requests to store messages without any CSRF token validation. Although Flask-WTF is in requirements.txt, no CSRFProtect is initialized and no CSRF tokens are included in forms. An attacker can craft a page that automatically submits a form to post messages on behalf of a victim.

Weak authorization check on confidential endpoint. The /confidential route only checks if session['loggedin'] exists (is truthy) but does not validate the session value against any user database or role. Combined with the /webmaster backdoor or forged session cookies (due to weak SECRET_KEY), any user can access confidential salary data.

Client-side constraint bypass. The Messaging navigation link is disabled using CSS only (pointer-events:none; opacity:0.6). There is no server-side access control on the /messaging endpoint, so an attacker can directly navigate to /messaging to access the messaging feature, bypassing the intended client-side restriction.

NOT vulnerable. Uses parameterized query with ? placeholders: db.execute('INSERT INTO messages(name, message, link, timestamp)VALUES(?,?,?,?)', (name, message, link, ...)). The user-supplied name, message, and link values are passed as parameters, preventing SQL injection.

NOT vulnerable. The /about GET endpoint loads pickle from a file (filename.pickle) that is written by the application itself with a hardcoded dictionary {'system_state': 'Synchronized with App2'}. Unlike the /about/sync POST endpoint, this pickle.load operates on application-controlled data, not user input.

NOT vulnerable for name and message fields. The template renders {{item[1]}} (name) and {{item[2]}} (message) with Jinja2's default autoescaping enabled (no autoescape false block). HTML special characters are automatically escaped to their entity equivalents, preventing XSS injection in these fields.

NOT vulnerable. The getMessages() function executes a static SQL query 'SELECT messageId, name, message, link, timestamp FROM messages' with no user-supplied input whatsoever. There is no injection vector.

The allowed_file() function itself is a proper security check that validates file extensions against a whitelist (txt, pdf, png, jpg, jpeg, html). It correctly uses rsplit to check the final extension. However, note that the XML_upload function that calls it is still vulnerable (defdev-app-009) because the filename is not sanitized for path traversal before saving.
